front: What is the formal definition of an open sphere (or open ball) in a metric space?
back: In a metric space (X, d), an open sphere S(x₀, r) centered at a point x₀ ∈ X with radius r > 0 is the set { x ∈ X : d(x₀, x) < r }.

front: What is the formal definition of a closed sphere (or closed ball) in a metric space?
back: In a metric space (X, d), a closed sphere S[x₀, r] centered at a point x₀ ∈ X with radius r > 0 is the set { x ∈ X : d(x₀, x) ≤ r }.

front: What is the key difference between an open sphere and a closed sphere?
back: The key difference is the inequality used in their definitions. An open sphere includes all points whose distance from the center is strictly less than r (d(x₀, x) < r), while a closed sphere includes all points whose distance is less than or equal to r (d(x₀, x) ≤ r).

front: In the standard Euclidean metric space ℝⁿ, what is the geometric shape of an open sphere?
back: In ℝⁿ with the standard Euclidean metric, an open sphere is the set of all points inside an n-dimensional sphere (e.g., a circle for n=2, a ball for n=3) but does not include the boundary itself.

front: Why is it important to note that a closed sphere is not necessarily the closure of an open sphere?
back: In a general metric space, the closure of an open sphere S(x₀, r) is not always equal to the closed sphere S[x₀, r]. The closed sphere S[x₀, r] is always a closed set, but the closure of S(x₀, r) may be a proper subset of S[x₀, r] in some unusual metric spaces.
